---
title: "ODE Solver"
---

ferx-core includes an adaptive Runge-Kutta solver for models whose structural
equations cannot be expressed analytically. It is invoked whenever a `.ferx`
model file contains an `[odes]` block; analytical PK models bypass it entirely.

## Algorithm: Dormand-Prince RK45

The solver implements the **Dormand-Prince** method (also called RK45 or
DOPRI5) — the same family as Julia's `Tsit5()`. It is an explicit, embedded
Runge-Kutta pair:

- **5th-order** solution used for advancing the state
- **4th-order** embedded solution used only for error estimation
- 7 function evaluations per step (the last stage is reused as the first of
  the next step — FSAL property)

The Butcher tableau coefficients are hardcoded in `ode/solver.rs` from the
original Dormand & Prince (1980) paper.

**Default tolerances:**

| Parameter | Default |
|---|---|
| `abstol` | `1e-6` |
| `reltol` | `1e-4` |
| `max_steps` | `10 000` |
| `initial_dt` | `0.1` |
| `min_dt` | `1e-12` |

These are appropriate for typical PK ODE systems (2–5 states, smooth dynamics,
concentration units in μg/mL or mg/L). Tighter tolerances can be set via
`[fit_options]` if needed.

## Adaptive step control

At each candidate step the solver computes the scaled error norm:

```
err = ‖(y₅ − y₄) / (abstol + reltol · |y₅|)‖₂ / √n
```

- If `err ≤ 1`: step accepted; next step size scaled up by `min(5, 0.9 · err^(-0.2))`
- If `err > 1`: step rejected and retried at `max(0.2, 0.9 · err^(-0.25)) · h`

The solver errors if `h` falls below `min_dt` (signals a stiff or ill-posed
system) or if the step count exceeds `max_steps`.

## Event handling

PK dose events create discontinuities in the ODE state that the solver cannot
step across. `ode/predictions.rs` handles this by:

1. Collecting all dose times and infusion-end times as **break points**
2. Integrating segment by segment between consecutive break points
3. At each bolus dose (EVID = 1), applying the state update: `u[cmt] += amt`
4. For infusions, adding `+rate` to the compartment derivative for the
   duration of the infusion via a closure wrapper around the user RHS

```rust
// Conceptual: infusion added to the RHS during [t_start, t_end]
let rhs_with_infusions = |u, params, t, du| {
    user_rhs(u, params, t, du);
    for (cmt_idx, rate) in &active_infusions {
        du[*cmt_idx] += rate;
    }
};
```

Break times are constructed so that each integration segment is either fully
inside or fully outside every infusion window — no float-equality check is
needed at the segment interior.

## Defining an ODE model

The `[odes]` section of a `.ferx` file specifies the right-hand side using a
simple expression DSL. Derivatives are written as `d/dt(STATE)`:

```
[odes]
  d/dt(depot)   = -KA * depot
  d/dt(central) = KA * depot - (CL / V) * central
```

Individual parameters from `[individual_parameters]` (CL, V, KA, …) are
available as symbols. The observable compartment is matched to the `CMT` column
in the data.

## When to use ODE vs analytical

| Situation | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| 1-cpt or 2-cpt oral/IV, standard dosing | Use analytical (`pk one_cpt_oral(...)`) |
| PD link model, transit compartments, enterohepatic circulation | Use `[odes]` |
| Saturable elimination (Michaelis-Menten), parallel pathways | Use `[odes]` |

Analytical models are typically 10–50× faster per likelihood evaluation and
support the Enzyme AD gradient path. ODE models currently fall back to
finite-difference gradients.

## Relevant source files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `ode/solver.rs` | `solve_ode()`, Dormand-Prince coefficients, adaptive step loop |
| `ode/predictions.rs` | `ode_predictions()`, event handling, break-time construction |
| `ode/mod.rs` | Module re-exports |
